specification.topic-statement: The message model represents LLM conversation turns as role-tagged arrays of typed content blocks and converts between the persistent database storage format and the provider request format.

scope.in-scope: The three message roles, the five content block types and their fields, message creation, conversion from database records to provider messages, and the handling of thinking blocks and tool call signatures.
scope.boundary: How messages are serialized into provider-specific JSON request bodies (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) is handled by the respective provider implementations and is out of scope.

The selection of which messages to include in a request based on token counts is managed by the token cache subsystem and is out of scope.
The token cache tracks per-turn token costs and prunes the oldest turns when the total exceeds the configured budget, advancing a context start index past the removed messages.
How messages are written to or read from the PostgreSQL database is managed by the database layer and is out of scope.

field.name: role
field.note: one of: user, assistant, tool

field.name: content blocks
field.note: ordered array; may contain one or more blocks

field.name: content count
field.note: number of blocks in the array

field.name: provider metadata
field.note: reserved JSON string for provider-specific extensions; null in all current cases

field.name: interrupted
field.note: true if the message was cancelled before completion

field.name: text
field.note: the message text; must not be null or empty

field.name: id
field.note: provider-assigned tool call identifier; must not be null

field.name: name
field.note: tool function name; must not be null

field.name: arguments
field.note: tool arguments as a JSON string; must not be null

field.name: thought signature
field.note: cryptographic signature from the model's thinking step; used for round-tripping; null for providers that do not supply it

field.name: tool call id
field.note: identifies the tool call this result answers; must not be null

field.name: content
field.note: tool execution output; must not be null

field.name: is error
field.note: true if the tool execution failed

field.name: text
field.note: the model's reasoning text

field.name: signature
field.note: cryptographic signature required to pass the thinking block back to the provider on subsequent turns

field.name: data
field.note: provider-encrypted opaque payload (base64-encoded); must be passed back verbatim

field.name: id
field.note: database row identifier; 0 if the record was not loaded from the database

field.name: kind
field.note: discriminates message type; see conversion behavior for all recognized values

field.name: content
field.note: human-readable text content for simple messages

field.name: data json
field.note: structured JSON payload for tool call and tool result messages

field.name: interrupted
field.note: preserved from the database record

behavior.message-roles:
Three roles are defined:
- **User**: messages submitted by the human operator. - **Assistant**: messages produced by the LLM, including text responses and tool call requests. - **Tool**: messages supplying tool execution results back to the LLM.
System prompts are not represented as messages. They are passed separately in the request structure and never appear in the message array.
behavior.content-block-ordering:
A single message may contain multiple content blocks. The typical combinations are:
- A user or assistant text message contains a single text block. - A tool call message from the assistant may contain: - A thinking block followed by a tool call block. - A thinking block, a redacted-thinking block, and a tool call block. - A redacted-thinking block followed by a tool call block. - A tool call block alone. - A tool result message contains a single tool result block.
Blocks within a message are ordered: thinking blocks precede redacted-thinking blocks, which precede the tool call block.
behavior.database-conversion:
Database records are converted to provider messages according to the `kind` field. The following kinds produce provider messages:
Kind `user`: produces an assistant-role message with a single text block containing the `content` field value.
The database kind is `user` but the resulting message role is user, as expected. The naming is consistent.
Kind `assistant`: produces an assistant-role message with a single text block containing the `content` field value.
Kind `tool_call`: produces an assistant-role message by parsing the `data_json` field. The JSON object contains:
- `tool_call_id`: the tool call identifier - `tool_name`: the tool function name - `tool_args`: the arguments JSON string - `thinking` (optional object): with `text` and `signature` fields - `redacted_thinking` (optional object): with a `data` field
If a `thinking` object is present, a thinking block is prepended. If a `redacted_thinking` object is present, a redacted-thinking block follows the thinking block. The tool call block is always last.
Kinds `tool_result` and `tool`: produce a tool-role message by parsing the `data_json` field. The JSON object contains:
- `tool_call_id`: the tool call identifier this result answers - `output`: the result content string - `success` (boolean): whether the tool succeeded
The `is_error` field on the tool result block is the logical inverse of `success`. If the `success` field is absent, `is_error` defaults to false.
Kind `system`: produces no message. System prompts are not included in the message array; the caller receives a null output and proceeds to populate the request's system-prompt field by other means.
Kinds `clear`, `mark`, `rewind`, `agent_killed`, `command`, and `fork` are metadata records. They do not produce provider messages. Whether and how the caller handles them is outside the scope of this specification.
behavior.allocation-failure: All message and content block allocations are performed with a hierarchical allocator. If any allocation fails, the process aborts immediately. There is no partial-allocation recovery path.
